ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific combination of dang gui roasted duck with roasted pork and rice; FOODON_03315595 (duck dish) used as the closest broader anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (cooked duck meat dish) is the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for the dang gui herb-marinated duck and roast pork rice combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps to this specific Singaporean mixed-protein rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of Dang Gui Roasted Duck and Roasted Pork Rice typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a bed of steamed white rice topped with sliced roasted duck, sliced siu yuk (roasted pork belly), and a ladle of dang gui-infused braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Dang Gui Roasted Duck & Roasted Pork Rice — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dang Gui Roasted Duck & Roasted Pork Rice (cr_id G00010693), which is a canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck and pork belly composition data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dang Gui Roasted Duck and Roasted Pork Rice. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine-type), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The duck and pork protein/fat components do not contribute meaningfully to GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 63.5; however, accounting for the protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating effect, a conservative GL of 45.5 is reported. Carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 g/100g × 350 g = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is in the borderline range for hypertension management; patients should request reduced gravy and remove roasted pork skin to limit sodium and saturated fat intake, while the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) provides partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base contributes a high glycaemic index (~72) and 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, posing a significant postprandial glucose load; individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice substitution, reduce portion size, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to attenuate glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from roasted pork belly skin and duck skin; sodium 231 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted pork belly (siu yuk) and duck skin contribute saturated fat (estimated ~1.4 g/100 g) and the dish provides 231 mg sodium per 100 g; cardiovascular disease patients should remove skin from both meats, request less braising sauce, and limit portion size to manage saturated fat and sodium load, while the dang gui herb component may offer mild vasodilatory benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dang Gui anti-inflammatory properties offset by saturated fat and AGE formation from roasting
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis) and ginger in the marinade provide documented anti-inflammatory compounds; however, roasting at high temperatures generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and roasted pork skin contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid, which may amplify inflammatory pathways — patients should remove skin and moderate consumption frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dang Gui Roasted Duck and Roasted Pork Rice (当归烤鸭烧肉饭) is a Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with sliced roasted duck and sliced siu yuk (Cantonese-style roasted pork belly), served with a fragrant braising sauce infused with dang gui (Angelica sinensis), a traditional Chinese medicinal herb prized for its warm, slightly bitter, and aromatic flavour profile. The duck is marinated and roasted with five-spice powder, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and dang gui, yielding tender, flavourful meat with crisp skin. The roasted pork belly contributes a contrasting crackling skin and rich, fatty layers. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7 g and iron at 2.55 mg. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management, partially offset by a high potassium content of 327 mg/100 g. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dang gui herb is associated with anti-inflammatory and circulatory benefits in traditional Chinese medicine. This dish is a popular lunch and dinner option at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops, reflecting the Cantonese roast-meat (siu mei) culinary tradition adapted with herbal medicinal influences common in Singapore's Chinese food culture.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010693, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck and pork belly composition data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. GI estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. All other values are HPB-sourced.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for roasted duck (NDB 05141) and roasted pork belly (NDB 10006) weighted by approximate dish composition, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific dang gui roasted duck and roasted pork rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is unusual for a duck and pork dish and may reflect a data entry limitation in the HPB database — confirmed zero per HPB source, but users should note that duck and pork typically contain cholesterol (~70–90 mg/100 g for cooked duck meat). 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (jasmine-type) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. Glycemic load per serving (350 g plate) calculated as (72 × 88.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 63.5 raw; conservative estimate of 45.5 applied to account for protein and fat co-ingestion attenuation. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains roasted pork (siu yuk).
